text_blocks.text: int fopen(const char* path, const char* flags); // "r", "rb", "wb+", etc

Opens the given file at the provided path with access depending on the provided flags. If the flags consist of a string with “r” in it, it will be read-only. If it contains a “w” it will be writable. If it contains a “b” it will not be interpreted as a text file, but as “binary” instead. If it is “w+”, it will completely overwrite the file. If it has an “a”, it will automatically write to the end of the existing file.

input.notes:
- this is a really common situation where ABI collisions occur, but it can happen in other ways:
	- trying to statically link a library compiled with a different compiler into your program
	- using the same compiler, but using different compilation options/flags for different parts of your program
	- using different versions of the same compiler for different parts of your program
	- using the wrong ABI for the OS you're compiling for

input.notes:
- it might be hard to believe, but there was a time when the idea of "functions calling other functions" was revolutionary.
	- and computers designed before this idea had no way to easily implement a call stack!
	- the performance of some HLLs suffered on these architectures just because pushing and popping things was so expensive.
- these example instructions are from x86. x86 has several stack instructions, but MIPS has none.
	- in fact MIPS doesn't even have a dedicated stack pointer register. any register could be used as one. we just agree to use $29.

input.notes:
- it's not that value types are harder. they're actually easier to understand in a lot of ways!
	- I've noticed a lot of beginner programmers expect everything to be value types and are surprised by how reference types work.
	- immutable strings are "reference types," but immutable reference types basically act like value types for practical purposes.
- "simplicity" is a bit misleading. it'll be simpler to deal with in the compiler, but…
	- then we have to deal with dynamic memory management. 
references give us another kind of indirection, and that can solve lots of problems.
but in languages like Java, every custom type has to be behind a reference, even tiny little objects that only contain a couple value types and which are created, used once, and then thrown away. super common to have a Point class in Java for things like GUIs and these Points are created and destroyed SO OFTEN.
this is slower (because of the need to access the items of the object indirectly); takes up more memory (because each object has some other hidden stuff stored in it that you don’t see, plus you have to store the reference in addition to the thing it references); and creates more pressure on the garbage collector (because now it has a lot more objects to worry about).

input.notes:
the slide title is a reference to Tony (aka C.A.R.) Hoare, who added null to Algol in 1965 “because it was easy to add” and it has ended up being a huge mistake in language design for decades since.
the “right” way to add null to a language is to either have something like Rust’s Option, or if you don’t want to go full “polymorphic types,” you can have a special syntax to just make reference types nullable. 
for example you could make it so the type “Object” can never be null, but the type “Object?” is possibly null, and you’d be forced to check it for null before using it.

text_blocks.text: unfortunately, lots of CPU architectures hate this.
CPUs like values to be aligned. that means: the memory address in an n-byte load/store must be a multiple of n.
x86 CPUs will access unaligned values very slowly. MIPS CPUs will crash. (unless you use the much slower unaligned loads/stores)

- therefore doing an unaligned access can "cross the boundary" between two of these groups of bytes…
	- forcing the memory circuitry to do two accesses to get one value.

input.title: Virtual method tables (vtables)

input.notes:
- every problem can be solved with another layer of indirection!
- by default each subclass's vtable starts as a copy of its superclass's.
	- like here, Cat and Dog both use Animal.breathe().
when you override methods, you are replacing entries in the vtable.

[diagram: assume there is a class Animal from which Cat and Dog both derive. the first field of each instance of Cat and Dog is a “virtual method table” pointer, or “v-table” pointer. Each class has its own v-table. Every instance of Cat will point to the Cat v-table, and every instance of Dog will point to the Dog v-table. The v-table contains pointers to every method declared in the class. Methods that are inherited from the base class – here, the methods “breathe” and “to_string” are inherited from Animal – will appear at the same position in every subclass’s v-table. So it does not matter if you call to_string on a Cat or a Dog or an Animal, the pointer to that method is always going to be at ”offset 4” in this example. now, each class can make their own implementations of each method, and all that means is that the v-table pointers are changed. here, Cat and Dog both use the default implementation of ”breathe” from the Animal class. but they also both have their own implementations of to_string, play_string, and play_fetch.]

jalr works like jal, but uses a register as the address of the function to call.

text_blocks.text: the compiler knows the method indexes because the vtables for each class use prefixing:

runs.text: the compiler knows the method indexes because the

text_blocks.text: subtypes' vtables are prefixed with their supertype's vtable.

input.notes:
so the vtables are like objects that inherit from one another, too!
the use of an indirect call instruction here implies something: for efficient virtual method dispatch, the target language needs some way to do an indirect call like this. (I’m not aware of any other equally-performant way of implementing them.)
this is something that even some modern microcontrollers struggle with, making virtual dispatch on those platforms less performant.

[diagram: a function that takes an Animal reference a and calls a.to_string(). The way that gets generated is something more like “a.vtbl[1](a)”, that is, it indexes the v-table using the index for “to_string” to get the pointer to that method; then it calls that method using “a” as the first argument so it becomes the “this” pointer in the method. in MIPS, this gets generated as a lw to get “a” off the stack; then a lw to get its v-table field like any other struct access; then a final lw to get the method out of the v-table, which is finally called using the “jalr” instruction. “jalr” calls a function whose address is in a register.]

	- the interface variable is a tuple of (object, offset) where "offset" is the beginning of the vtable slice
	- to call interface method n on it, you do object.vtable[offset + n](object)

- multiple inheritance is of questionable utility and is really hard to implement "right" in a compiled language.
	- Python can get away with it because of its dynamic nature, but even then, it's confusing to the programmer

- "Sufficiently Smart Compiler" is a sort of a meme in CS; there have been many programming languages and ISAs whose performance rely on a "sufficiently smart compiler," a compiler that is complex enough to "figure everything out" correctly. a lot of the time, such a compiler is prohibitively difficult or impossible to create, meaning "sufficiently smart compiler" becomes a meaningless hand-wave or marketing buzz.

	- the IR is an abstract representation of the program's behavior.

[diagram: the frontend of the compiler does the lexing, parsing, and semantic analyses. but now, instead of it outputting the AST directly to the backend, the last step of the frontend is a lowering pass which takes the AST and outputs an intermediate representation, or IR. the IR is then sent to the backend or backends plural. now each backend only has to worry about converting the much simpler IR to the target language – MIPS or x86 or ARM or whatever; and the lowering pass only has to convert the source language AST to the still-relatively-high-level IR, which is much easier than going all the way to a real machine language.]

[diagram: a simple if looks like a “detour” – a conditional block where, when the condition is true, we run the body; but if the condition is false, and after the body runs, we go to the “next” block. a while loop looks almost the same, except after running the body, we come back to the condition.an infinite loop is a block that unconditionally goes to itself.an if-else if-else chain “branches out” into several paths, where each condition is checked in turn, but eventually one of the paths is chosen; and all the paths then merge back into a single block that is “after” the if-else if-else.
finally a C-style for loop (like “for(i = 0; i < 10; i++)”) has a block that initializes the counter; that unconditionally goes to the condition-checking block; when the condition is true, we go to the body block, which then goes to the increment block, which then comes back to the condition block; and finally when the condition is false, we go to the “block after the loop.”]
